briefing.opening: Good morning. I will now begin the daily climate change briefing for June 17, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Urgent Warning: Deepening Climate Crisis and the Ocean's Warning
sections.content: According to reports today, the rate of climate change in Asia is progressing faster than the global average, leading to record-high heat accumulation in the oceans [1]. While the oceans have historically protected humanity from the worst impacts of climate change, they are now 'suffering from a fever' themselves, with an analysis showing that marine heatwave days in 2025 have increased by more than three times compared to the early 1990s [48, 94]. As global warming approaches a 2-degree Celsius increase, scientists are warning of a surge in heatwaves this summer [53], and indeed, domestically, 297 heat-related illnesses have occurred in just one month, a 1.5-fold increase from last year [45]. Furthermore, rising temperatures can increase the phenomenon of 'hydroclimatic whiplash' – rapid shifts between heavy rainfall and prolonged drought in rivers – thereby elevating flood risks [92, 62, 63, 66], and children in the poorest countries, such as Afghanistan, are facing severe threats from floods, droughts, and heatwaves [54, 67, 85].

sections.title: ⚠️ Challenges of Energy Transition and Policy Dilemmas
sections.content: In the process of energy transition towards carbon neutrality, several policy and economic challenges are emerging. Concerns have been raised that the lack of electricity tariff reduction benefits for 'electric furnaces,' a key technology for carbon neutrality, could hinder investment attraction in related industries [9, 22]. Furthermore, the 'energy mix' policy, which aims to simultaneously expand nuclear power and renewable energy in response to surging electricity demand, is raising concerns about potential conflicts among experts, and criticism suggests that certain regions could become sacrificial victims of energy policies [27, 46]. There are also growing calls to move away from sole reliance on renewable energy, redefine the concept of energy security, and address vulnerabilities in electricity tariffs and renewable energy supply chains [37, 44]. Notably, the surge in silver prices due to the development of AI technology is causing an emergency in the cost structure of the solar power industry [40], and analyses indicating China's dominance over the renewable energy supply chain add to the complexity of the international energy market [31, 59].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Climate Action and Carbon Neutrality Efforts
sections.content: Diverse domestic and international efforts to address climate change are actively underway. Domestically, climate change adaptation efforts in the agriculture and forestry sectors are notable, including Anseong City's research on selecting glutinous corn varieties [2, 3] and Suncheon City's on-site agricultural training [5]. Gwangmyeong City and Gwangju City are encouraging citizen participation through data-driven green building policy leadership and carbon neutrality experiential education commemorating Environment Day, respectively [11, 12, 20, 24, 25], and examples of carbon neutrality practices in daily life, such as Anseong Facilities Management Corporation's 'Plastic Exchange Station' [13] and Uiseong County's 'Used Battery Exchange Education' [14], are also spreading. In the energy sector, Korea Southern Power has announced plans to supply 11.2 GW of renewable energy by 2040 [28, 29, 41], and large-scale investments are being made, with the Korea Energy Agency pursuing the creation of a 350 billion KRW renewable energy fund [30]. The adoption of innovative technologies is also active, including efficiency innovations in power plant operations using AI [17, 32, 34] and the operation of the nation's largest solar power complex [35]. Furthermore, led by Chairman Lee Chan-woo, NH NongHyup Financial Group is pursuing a 'Green Drive' to expand new businesses in renewable energy and carbon finance [38].

sections.title: 📰 Climate Change Research and Adaptation Strategies
sections.content: Research and discussions are actively underway to understand the complexities of climate change and explore effective adaptation strategies. Interesting research findings have been published, indicating that ancient innovative technologies, such as Benjamin Franklin's lightning rod, are still helping scientists understand climate change centuries later [50, 56, 58]. Scientists have convened to discuss high-altitude climate change [51], and research suggesting that climate change can increase the size of beans but decrease their nutritional value raises new concerns about food security [61]. Furthermore, research repeatedly emphasizes the greater need for locally-led approaches to climate change adaptation [52, 88]. Observations that climate change affects women's health and that climate finance is not gender-sensitive highlight issues of equity in climate action [55, 70]. Meanwhile, analyses showing that climate change is driving up insurance premiums demonstrate its economic ripple effects [81], and research indicating that traditional legal systems are inadequate for the realities of climate change suggests the need for institutional innovation [89].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlights the severity of the climate crisis, alongside the diverse domestic and international efforts underway to overcome it. This is a time that demands urgent action and continuous innovation. This concludes today's climate briefing.
